subject: Literature in English
q: Essentially,plays are meant
options: to be read for sheer pleasure
to be presented on stage through words and actions
to keep people out of trouble
explanation: A play is a form of dramatic literature that is specifically written to be performed rather than just read. It uses dialogue, actions, stage directions and character interaction to convey its message and entertain or educate an audience. Plays are staged in theatres with actors performing the script. The visual and auditory elements (movement, tone, set design) are essential to their full impact.

subject: Literature in English
q:
In one of the three jugs
The three jugs were on certain evenings to return the tranquil souls,
the breaths of the ancestors,
The ancestors who were men,
The ancestors who were sages,
Mother has dipped three fingers
three fingers of her left hand:
thumb, forefinger and middle finger
I have dipped three fingers
Three fingers of my right hand:
thumb, forefinger and middle finger.
(From "Viaticum"by B. Diop)
In this poem, the repetitive pattern suggests
options: ritual or established form of worship
the love of a mother for her child
the beauty of the full moon
the glorious lives of our ancestors
explanation:
The poem "Viaticum" by B. Diop uses repetition, particularly in the actions of dipping three fingers and the naming of those fingers, to evoke a ceremonial or sacred atmosphere. This kind of repetition is characteristic of rituals or traditional forms of worship, especially when tied to ancestral reverence.

  • The mention of ancestors, tranquil soul and the specific, repeated action involving dipping fingers all indicate a ritualistic gesture.

  • Such patterns are common in traditional African religious or spiritual practices, where actions are symbolic, deliberate and repetitive to maintain sacred form.

subject: Literature in English
q: The language of David Diop's 'Africa' suggests
options: the beauty of the African country side
that the poet is sad because Africa has been exploited and laid waste for too long
that the poet remembers the great empire of Africa
that the poet sees Africa as a colonial power
explanation:
David Diop’s poem ‘Africa is a lamentation of the suffering and exploitation that Africa has endured over the centuries, particularly under the yoke of colonialism.

The poem is not about the beauty of the African countryside (option A) or the greatness of an African empire (option D). Rather, it is a deeply emotional and personal reflection on the pain and suffering that Africa has experienced throughout its history. The poet’s language is not celebratory or triumphant, but rather mournful and sorrowful, as he reflects on the many injustices that have been inflicted upon the continent and its people.

Option B, “that black is beautiful,” is not a theme that is explicitly addressed in the poem. While the poet does celebrate the beauty of Africa and its people, he does not do so in terms of skin colour or race. Rather, his focus is on the land itself and the people who inhabit it.

Option E, “that the poet sees Africa as a colonial power,” is also not a theme that is addressed in the poem. Rather, the poem is a critique of colonialism and the damage that it has inflicted upon Africa and its people.

Therefore, option C, “that the poet is sad because Africa has been exploited and laid waste for too long,” is the most accurate answer to this question. The poem is a powerful and moving reflection on the pain and suffering that Africa has experienced throughout its history, and the poet’s language is rich and evocative as he explores these themes.

subject: Literature in English
q: In 'The Castle' Muir writes:Our only enemy was gold'! This is true because
options: the castle was made of gold
there was no gold to pay for arms
the captain stole all available gold
gold was the cause of their betrayal
explanation:
The poem tells the story of the unexpected defeat of an empire. In the beginning, the narrator builds an atmospheres of confidence within the castle before the invasion, one that would lead the reader to assume the soldiers who were at the castle never suspected that such a fall was possible. However, the soldiers were betrayed by one of their own, the warder, who let the enemy through a small gate after being bribed. 

Hence the last two stanza reads;

''Our only enemy was gold,
And we had no arms to fight it with.''

subject: Literature in English
q:
You cannot know
And should not bother;
Tide and market come and go
And so shall your mother.
In this verse the poet uses
explanation:
The rhyme scheme in the verse is:

You cannot know (A)
And should not bother; (B)
Tide and market come and go (A)
And so shall your mother. (B)

This follows an ABAB pattern, which is known as alternate rhyme.

A monorhyme means all lines end with the same rhyme (AAAA). Couplets consist of rhyming pairs (AABB, etc.). A triplet consists of three lines that rhyme together (AAA, BBB). A blank verse consists of unrhymed lines, usually in iambic pentameter.

subject: Literature in English
q:
The Comstocks belonged to the most dismal of all classes,the middle-middle class,the landless gentry.In their miserable poverty they had not even the snobbish consolation of regarding themselves as an 'old' family fallen on evil days.....

This writer's tone is
explanation:
The description of the Comstocks is filled with mocking irony:

“the most dismal of all classes”
“miserable poverty”
“not even the snobbish consolation…”

The writer is not simply sad. He is ridiculing the pretensions and social position of the “middle-middle class.” The phrase “snobbish consolation” especially shows sarcasm. The humour is sharp and critical, not light-hearted. That makes the tone bitterly humorous i.e. humour mixed with criticism.

subject: Literature in English
q: An image in poetry usually gives a clue to one of the following:
explanation:
An image in poetry refers to descriptive language that appeals to the senses—sight, sound, touch, taste, or smell. It helps readers visualise scenes, emotions, or ideas, and often provides insight into the underlying message or theme of the poem.

Poets use imagery to illustrate or reinforce central ideas. For example, images of decay might reflect themes of death or impermanence; images of light might suggest hope or truth. Thus, when analysing a poem, examining the imagery often reveals the theme the poet is exploring.

subject: Literature in English
q: One of the peculiar features of drama in general is
explanation: One of the most peculiar (essential) features of drama is action because: Drama is primarily a performative genre, meaning it is meant to be acted out on stage or screen rather than just read. Unlike other literary forms like poetry or prose, drama relies on dialogue and physical movement to tell the story. The plot unfolds through characters interacting and engaging in events, making action a fundamental element of drama.

subject: Literature in English
q: Dialogue is important in drama because
options: it always makes the audience laugh
it reveals the minds of the characters
it makes the play last longer on the stage
explanation: Dialogue is important in drama because it allows characters to express their thoughts, emotions, desires and motivations. Through dialogue, audiences can gain insights into the inner workings of characters' minds, understand their conflicts, and connect with their experiences. Effective dialogue helps create depth and complexity in characters and make them more relatable and engaging for the audience.

subject: Literature in English
q: A good poem must
explanation: A good poem doesn't necessarily need to be about love, have a regular rhythm, rhyme or be difficult to understand. Poetry is a versatile art form that can take many shapes and styles. For example, free verse poems often lack rhyme or regular rhythm but can still be powerful and meaningful. Similarly, poems can explore a wide range of themes beyond love, and their beauty often lies in their accessibility and relatability.

q:
All this was a long time ago,i remember
And i would do it again,but set down
This set down
This:were we led all that way for
Birth and Death?There was a Birth,certainly,
We had evidence and no doubt.I had seen birth and death,
But had thought they were different;this birth was
Hard and bitter agony for us,like Death,our death
We returned to our places,these kingdoms,
But no longer at ease here,in the old dispensation,
With an alien people clutching their gods.
I should be glad of another death.
(From T.S Elliott's 'Journey Of The Magi')
The Magi are no longer at ease because
options: they suffered alot on their way to Bethlehem
they found greater spiritual satisfaction from their journey
death and birth are one and the same thing
their kingdoms can no longer offer them satisfaction
the world has changed for the worse
explanation:
The Magi express a sense of disillusionment in the final lines of the stanza, stating:

We returned to our places, these kingdoms,
But no longer at ease here, in the old dispensation,
With an alien people clutching their gods.

This suggests that, after their transformative journey, the Magi no longer feel comfortable or at peace in their previous kingdoms. The experience has altered their perceptions, and they find it hard to reconcile with the old ways or the old beliefs.

subject: Literature in English
q:
New Year's Eve Midnight
Now the bells are tolling-
A year is dead.
And my heart is slowly beating
the Nunc Dimittis
to all my hopes and mute
yewnings of a year
and ghosts hover round
dream beyond dream

Dream beyond dream
mingling with the dying
bell-sounds fading
into memories
like rain drops
falling into a river.

And now the bells are chimming-
A year is born.
And my heart-bell is ringing
in dawn
But it's shrouded things i see
dimly stride
on heart-canopied paths
to a riverside
The mood of the above poem is
options: much optimism qualified by a little pessimism
much pessimism lightened by a little optimism
explanation:
The poem "New Year's Eve Midnight" captures the transition from one year to another and reflects on past regrets and uncertain future hopes. Let's break down the mood using specific lines:

 Pessimism (dominant tone):

  • "A year is dead."

    • A stark, final statement—death metaphor conveys a sense of loss or failure.

  • "Nunc Dimittis to all my hopes..."

    • Nunc Dimittis is a Latin hymn associated with death and letting go—indicates resignation or mourning for unfulfilled dreams.

  • "ghosts hover round / dream beyond dream"

    • Evokes haunting memories and regrets.

  • "shrouded things I see / dimly stride"

    • Suggests an uncertain, unclear future—shrouded, dimly seen, not hopeful.

i must hear none!These misted calls will yet

Here once lay Keta
Now her golden girls
Erode into the arms
of strange towns.

q:
From the West
Clouds come hurrying with the wind
Turning
Sharply
Here and there
Like a plague of locusts
Whirling
Tossing up things on its tail
Like a madman chasing nothing

Pregnant clouds
Ride stately on its back
Gathering to perch on hills
Like dark sinister wings:
The wind whistles by
And trees bend to let it pass

In the village
Screams of delighted children
Toss and turn
In the dim of whirling wind
Women-
Babies clinging to their backs-
Dart about
In and out
Madly
The wind whistles by
Whilst trees bend to let it pass.

In those days
When civilization kicked us in the face
When holy water slapped our cringing brows
The vultures built in the shadow of their talons
The blood stained monument of tutelage
In those days
There was painful laughter on the metallic hell
of the roads
And the monotonous rythm of the paternoster
Drowned the howling of the plantations
Of the bitter memories of the extorted kisses
Of promises broken at the point of a gun

Nor our hands which fertilize the womb of the earth

Inspite of the desolate villages of Africa torn apart
Hope lived in us like a citadel
And from Swaziland's mines to the sweltering sweat
of Europe's factories
Spring will be reborn under our bright steps.

subject: Literature in English
q: Literature is studied as a subject at school because
options: it exposes students to the realities of life
it merely gives additional work to students
it teaches the use of words
it provides a means to kill time
explanation:
Literature is studied in schools not just for reading or entertainment, but because it:

  • Reflects real-life situations and human experiences: Through stories, poetry and drama, literature portrays human struggles, emotions, cultures and moral questions.

  • Encourages empathy and critical thinking: Students understand diverse perspectives and develop deeper insight into societal issues.

  • Promotes awareness of values and human behaviour: It helps students examine moral choices and cultural practices, leading to self-reflection.

  • Stimulates imagination and creativity: While it can entertain, its primary educational role is far deeper.

Although options B and D are partly true (literature does entertain and involves the use of language), A best captures the core educational purpose of studying literature in schools.

The injustice is symbolized by various aspects of the author's experiences and observations. However, the sign Reserved for Europeans Only' symbolizes a clear segregation and discrimination based on race and highlights the deep-rooted injustices prevalent during that time period.

q: Wordsworth's 'The World is Too Much With Us' is a
explanation:
William Wordsworth's "The World is Too Much With Us" is a sonnet. A sonnet is a 14-line poem written in iambic pentameter, typically with a specific rhyme scheme. This poem follows the structure of a Petrarchan sonnet, consisting of an octave (eight lines) and a sestet (six lines), with a rhyme scheme of ABBAABBA for the octave and  CDCDCD for the sestet.

The poem critiques the materialism of the modern world and laments humanity's disconnection from nature.

subject: Literature in English
q: 'The pen is mightier than the sword' is an example of
explanation:
“The pen is mightier than the sword” is a famous phrase that means the written word is more powerful than physical force. This phrase is an example of metonymy, a figure of speech that replaces words with related or associated words. In this case, “pen” is used to represent the written word, and “sword” is used to represent physical force.

Metonymy is a figure of speech in which one object or idea takes the place of another with which it has a close association.

subject: Literature in English
q: As a literary from the short story is most closely related to
explanation:
The short story is most closely related to the novel (option D) because both are forms of prose fiction that focus on narrative storytelling. Here's an explanation of each option:

A. Poetry: Poetry is a form of literature characterized by rhythmic and condensed language that employs metre and rhyme. Short stories and novels, on the other hand, are typically written in prose and focus on telling a story and developing characters rather than on poetic elements.

B. The Discourse: This term is broad and could refer to any form of written or spoken communication. It doesn't specifically highlight the narrative and fictional elements that define short stories and novels.

C. Tragedy: Tragedy is a genre typically associated with drama. While short stories and novels can certainly include tragic elements, they are not all tragedies.

D. The Novel: The novel is a long-form prose narrative that allows for an in-depth exploration of characters, themes, and plots. The short story shares similarities with the novel in terms of narrative structure and storytelling but is typically shorter and focuses on a single, central theme or idea.

E. Drama: Drama refers to plays and scripts written for performance on stage. While short stories and novels share some narrative elements with drama, they are distinct forms of literary expression.

subject: Literature in English
q:
The bustle in a house
The morning after death
Is solemnest of industries
Enacted upon earth-
The sweeping up the heart
And putting love away
We shall not want to use again
Until eternity.
The predominant mood of this poem is best captured by the words
explanation:
The poem by Emily Dickinson describes the quiet and sorrowful activities that follow a death, emphasizing grief and acceptance.

  • "The bustle in a house / The morning after death" – This suggests the somber routine of dealing with loss.

  • "The sweeping up the heart / And putting love away" – This metaphorically represents coming to terms with the loss and setting aside emotions.

  • "We shall not want to use again / Until eternity" – This implies acceptance of grief until a reunion in the afterlife.

The mood is one of solemn resignation, meaning a quiet acceptance of grief.

q: At the end of the poem 'pianos and Drums', the protagonist is best described as
explanation: In Gabriel Okara's poem "Pianos and Drums," the protagonist at the end of the poem is best described asma cultural mulatto. The term "cultural mulatto" here refers to the blending or intersection of Western and African cultural influences, which is a key theme in the poem. The speaker grapples with the conflict between the allure of Western influences (represented by the pianos) and the traditional African heritage (represented by the drums). The term "mulatto" is used metaphorically to convey this blending or mixing of cultural elements in the protagonist's identity.

explanation: In Leopold Senghor's poem "Long long you have held between your hands," the word "storms" metaphorically represents the hardships and struggles faced by the people, including oppressive actions such as raids or attacks by racist police forces. It does not refer to literal weather phenomena like sea or rain storms, nor does it symbolise storms of passion. Senghor often uses vivid imagery and metaphors to highlight the challenges of colonial oppression and the resilience of his people

explanation:
The speaker of these lines is Baroka, the Bale (chief) of Ilujinle, in The Lion and the Jewel by Wole Soyinka. He tells Sadiku, his senior wife, that he has become impotent. However, this is later revealed to be a trick to manipulate Sidi, the village belle, into marrying him.

By spreading the false news of his impotence through Sadiku, Baroka plays on Sidi’s emotions, making her feel confident and in control. However, he eventually seduces her, proving that his claim is a deceptive strategy rather than the truth. This shows Baroka's cunning nature and ability to manipulate situations to his advantage, making option C (a crafty seducer) the best choice.

explanation:
A paradox is a statement that appears self-contradictory or illogical but may reveal a deeper truth upon reflection. In the lines from The City of Dreams by Robert Buchanan:

"I die, yet depart not,"

"I am bound, yet soar free;"

"Thou art and thou art not,"

Each statement presents a contradiction. The speaker claims to simultaneously experience opposing states—dying but not departing, being bound yet free, and existing while not existing. These contradictions challenge conventional logic, making the passage a clear example of a paradox.

q:

The Bale is symbolically referred to as a fox because of his
explanation:
In Wole Soyinka’s The Lion and the Jewel,  Lakunle, the schoolteacher, refers to Baroka, the Bale (traditional village chief), as a fox. This metaphor symbolises Baroka’s cunning, cleverness and strategic thinking.

This happened when Sidi, Sadiku and Lakunle were having a conversation. Sidi tells them that she is familiar with Baroka's "little supper" that he uses to lure unsuspecting maidens to his bed. It is then that Lakunle says: "Is it for nothing he is called the Fox?" (p.23). Lakunle narrates how Baroka had bribed a white surveyor not to allow the railway to pass through the village so that he could continue to maintain his power over his people. This suggests that the label Fox symbolises Baroka's mental prowess. 

The fox is a classic symbol of craftiness and mental agility, not necessarily physical strength or sexual ability, although Baroka does display some of those traits. However, in this context, the emphasis is on his ability to manipulate and outwit others.

q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles and Appreciation.

'If it is true that the world talks too much
then let's all keep quiet
and hear the eloquence
of silence'
If it is True' by Richard Ntini
One striking device used by the poet above is
explanation:
In Richard Ntini's poem "If it is True", the poet contrasts "talking too much" with the idea of "keeping quiet" to highlight the power and meaning of silence. This use of opposing ideas is a clear example of contrast, a literary device that shows the difference between two concepts to emphasise one or both.

Line-by-line Analysis:

"If it is true that the world talks too much
then let's all keep quiet*
and hear the eloquence*
of silence"*

  • The world’s noise (talking too much) is set against the quiet (keeping silent).

  • The unexpected twist is calling silence "eloquent", which intensifies the contrast—silence vs. speech.

When a story is told in a song and passed down by word of mouth, it is called
explanation: When a story is told in a song and passed down by word of mouth, it is called a ballad. A ballad is a narrative poem or song that tells a story, often of a romantic or sentimental nature, and is passed down orally from one generation to the next. It is usually composed of quatrains (four-line stanzas) with a rhyme scheme of ABAB or ABCB. Ballads are often accompanied by music and are a popular form of folk poetry.

Comic relief is
options: the use of humorous characters speeches or scenes in a tragic work
a figure of speech used in comic theatre
the use of hearty and wholesome laughter in plays
a device used in the theatre where a play ends in marriage
explanation:
Comic relief refers to the inclusion of humorous elements, characters, or scenes within a primarily serious or tragic work, with the intention of providing relief to the audience from the emotional intensity or tension of the main plot. The purpose of comic relief is to offer moments of laughter or light-heartedness amidst the more somber or intense themes of a tragedy or dramatic work.

So, option A accurately describes comic relief as the use of humorous elements within a tragic work to provide relief from the seriousness of the main plot.

John Keats, 'Ode to a Nightingale'. The above lines are an example of
explanation:
Consonance is the repetition of consonant sounds, especially at the end or within words.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on Literary Principles.
'Still stand stubborn
To stones that strangle the dawn,
Still stand stubborn
To stones that maim the morn,
still stand stubborn
To stones that assail the sun'
Atukwei Okai, 'Sunset Sonata'
The dominant literary device employed in the above passage is
explanation:
Alliteration is a literary device where a series of words in a sentence or phrase share the same initial consonant sound. In the provided passage, the repetition of the initial "s" sound in the phrase "Still stand stubborn" and throughout the lines is an example of alliteration. This repetitive use of the same consonant sound creates a musical and rhythmic quality. It draws attention to the words and emphasises the defiance and resilience expressed in the poem.

While repetition is present in the passage, the more prominent and distinctive feature is the alliteration. The title of the poem, "Sunset Sonata," also exhibits alliteration with the repeated "s" sound and this reinforces the stylistic choice of the poet.

"Here lies our sovereign Lord the King,
Whose word no man relies on,
Who never said a foolish thing
Nor ever did a wise one,"

Based on John Wilmot Rochester's Epitaph on King Charles 11, the form of the above stanza is an example of
explanation:
An epigram is a brief, witty, and often paradoxical statement or poem that expresses an idea in a clever or satirical way.

 

In this stanza, John Wilmot, Earl of Rochester, makes a sharp and ironic comment about King Charles II. The paradox in the last two lines—"Who never said a foolish thing / Nor ever did a wise one"—is a classic example of an epigrammatic style, as it presents a witty contradiction.

Which of the following are literary genres?
explanation:
The correct answer is B. Fiction, poetry, and drama are literary genres
- Fiction: This includes novels, short stories, and other narratives that are created from the imagination.
- Poetry: This includes lyrical and narrative poems, where language is used for its aesthetic and evocative qualities.
- Drama: This includes plays, scripts, and performances designed for the stage, often involving dialogue and acting.

The other options don't include a combination of recognized literary genres.

The Christs
are still in performing miracles
in the market places
heroding the masses with imperatives
feeding the hungry
with 21-gun salutes
for victories that are yet to be won.'
Odia Ofeimun, 'The Messiahs'
The attitude of the poet to his subject in the lines above is
explanation: Sarcasm is a form of verbal irony that involves using language to convey the opposite of what is actually meant, often with a mocking or scornful tone. In the poem, phrases like "heroding the masses with imperatives" suggest that the leaders are acting like King Herod, known for his authoritarian and oppressive rule, thus ridiculing their style of governance. Similarly, "feeding the hungry with 21-gun salutes" sarcastically implies that the leaders are celebrating symbolic victories or gestures instead of addressing real issues such as hunger and poverty.

The figures of speech employed in the lines above are
explanation:
• Simile – A simile is a comparison using "like" or "as."

    • In the line "shaven clean as a potato," the man's bald head is being compared to a potato using "as," making this a simile.

  • Metaphor – A metaphor is a direct comparison between two unrelated things without using "like" or "as."

    • In "the rising sun, a red eye wiped by a tattered handkerchief of clouds," the sun is metaphorically described as a red eye, and the clouds are metaphorically compared to a tattered handkerchief, making this a metaphor.

q:
This question is based on Buchi Emecheta's Second Class Citizen.
In Adah's view, the white society in the novel is one in which
explanation:
In Second Class Citizen by Buchi Emecheta, Adah, the protagonist, experiences the harsh realities of being an immigrant in Britain. She finds that the white society is largely individualistic, and there is a lack of warmth and communal support compared to the close-knit community she was used to in Nigeria. This shows that the members of the community are unfriendly towards immigrants.

In Buchi Emecheta's Second Class Citizen, Adah’s experience with the white society reflects a lack of neighbourliness and a sense of isolation. Several key moments in the novel support this view:

  1. Adah's Isolation in the Neighborhood:
    When Adah and her family first move to England, they are faced with a society that is indifferent and often hostile to them. Their neighbours show little interest in befriending them. Adah notes the lack of warmth or communal bonds in the area. This is reflected in her initial struggles to connect with those around her. The coldness of her neighbours leaves her feeling isolated, even though she is physically surrounded by people.

  2. Racial Prejudice and Segregation:
    Throughout the novel, Adah experiences racism and prejudice, which further underscores the lack of neighbourliness. Her efforts to engage with the white society are often met with rejection or indifference. This is reflected in the difficulty she had while searching for a new apartment in London as everywhere she went, the sign that read "Sorry, No Coloureds" was posted.  It is also reflected in the fact that Trudy was able to get away with the negligence of the children placed under her care, even when there was glaring evidence that she was a bad caretaker.

These examples illustrate that Adah’s view of white society in Second Class Citizen is one in which neighbourliness is indeed lacking, as racial prejudice and indifference create barriers to connection and communal feeling.

explanation: Titubi's experience at the peasants' camp is narrated through the technique of flashback, where the past events are recounted, often to provide context or reveal significant details about a character's experiences or motivations.

explanation: Osofisan makes use of a lot of metaphors in the play to give force and meaning to his expression. It helps to invite the readers into the story and make the lines more memorable an example of metaphor used in the play: "the long eye of the sky".

subject: Literature in English
q: The images depicted are those of
explanation: The images depicted in the speech are ones of hardship, as the speaker describes the lack of basic amenities (no electricity, drinking water from the stream), the imprisonment of children and the brutal actions of the police (maiming and destroying homes). These reflect suffering and brutality.

explanation: The speech is made by Mama Kayode role-playing Baba.

subject: Literature in English
q:
X: What country, friends, is this?
Y: This is lllyria, lady.
X: And what should I do in lllyria?
My brother he is in Elysium.
Perchance he is not drown' d;....

The dialogue above takes place
explanation: This dialogue occurs in Act 1, Scene 2 of Twelfth Night when Viola, disguised as Cesario, speaks to the Captain. After surviving the shipwreck, Viola is asking about her location, and the Captain informs her that they are in Illyria. This scene is set near the sea-coast of Illyria, as Viola is coming to terms with her brother’s apparent death and the new environment she finds herself in.

subject: Literature in English
q: The author successfully conveys his message through
explanation: Twelfth Night is a prime example of a comedy of errors, a genre characterized by misunderstandings, mistaken identities and complex situations that lead to humorous outcomes. The play revolves around a series of misunderstandings, particularly related to disguise and mistaken identity (such as Viola's cross-dressing as Cesario and the confusion it causes), which leads to comedic situations and ultimately a resolution.

subject: Literature in English
q:
Valentine:
....But, like a cloistress, she will veiled walk,
And water once a day her chamber round
With eve-offending brine; all this to season...

The imagery above describes
options: Viola's deep sorrow for her drowned brother
Olivia's deep sorrow over the loss of her brother's love
Viola's grief over a love she cannot show
Olivia's intense grief over the death of her brother
explanation: In this passage from Twelfth Night, Valentine describes Olivia's mourning ritual for her deceased brother. She isolates herself, refuses to interact with the world and indulges in daily rituals of mourning, including the "eve-offending brine" (tears).

subject: Literature in English
q: The play is about love
explanation: Twelfth Night revolves around themes of love, commitment and marriage. The play explores different types of love, including romantic love (as seen in the relationships of Viola and Orsino, and Olivia and Sebastian), self-love (Malvolio's vanity), and familial love (the bond between Viola and her twin brother, Sebastian). The play ultimately concludes with marriages.

subject: Literature in English
q: Two major elements of the diction in the play are the
options: political and educational background of players
gender and class of the speakers
status and emotion of the speakers
explanation: In Twelfth Night, Shakespeare uses diction (word choice) to reveal the status and emotions of the characters. The language reflects the social class and individual emotions of the characters and influences how they interact with one another. For example, characters like Malvolio use more formal, serious language to assert their position, while others like Feste, the fool, use wit and wordplay to reflect their lower status and playful emotional state.

subject: Literature in English
q: The success of the novel lies in the author's
options: realistic portrayal of Laye's courtship with Marie
effective use of sociological and fictional materials
recreation of an African childhood in Conakry
use of traditional elements for colour
explanation: Camera Laye combines sociological and fictional elements in the novel. He combines elements of his childhood and the childhood of most African children and imbues some fictional elements into the storytelling. The novel is celebrated for capturing the essence of traditional African life, cultural practices, and the emotional journey of growing up in an African society. Laye uses his personal experiences to convey a rich, evocative picture of life in Guinea.

subject: Literature in English
q: The most important objects in the hut are the
explanation: In the novel, the pot with the magic charms is the family's link to their ancestors. The magic charms are believed to offer protection and power.

subject: Literature in English
q: The friendship between Laye and Marie lasted because
options: they had mutual respect for each other
explanation: In The African Child by Camara Laye, Laye and Marie's friendship endures because of the mutual respect and understanding they share. Their bond is built on their connection and the respect they have for each other as individuals, not merely on external factors such as their school relationship, physical appearance or approval from others. This respect plays a key role in the development of their friendship throughout the story.

subject: Literature in English
q: The novel is an expose of the
options: pre-eminent position of boys in lgboland
experiences of Adah's mother in the police cell
explanation: Second Class Citizen by Buchi Emecheta explores the challenges faced by Adah, the protagonist, as she navigates life as a girl-child in a patriarchal society. The novel exposes the discrimination and hardships she endures due to her gender, especiall in the context of her upbringing in Igboland and her later experiences in London. It highlights the social inequalities and struggles that many girls face, particularly in relation to education, marriage and societal expectations.

subject: Literature in English
q: For her act of child neglect, Adah's mother was forced by the police to
options: pay a fine of ten shillings
explanation: In Second Class Citizen by Buchi Emecheta, Adah’s mother faces punishment from the police for neglecting her child, Adah. As a form of punishment, she is made to drink a bowl of garri which causes her great discomfort.

subject: Literature in English
q: Adah's sojourn in London reveals that
options: marriage  is an equal partnership between spouses
the plight of the black woman does not change
explanation: In Second Class Citizen by Buchi Emecheta, Adah's experience in London highlights the struggles and challenges she faces as a black woman. Despite her dreams of a better life and the promises of a new environment, she encounters racism, sexism and the oppressive limitations placed on her because of her gender and race.

subject: Literature in English
q: The old man's experience is a lesson in
explanation: In The Old Man and the Sea, Santiago’s experience is a profound lesson in perseverance, endurance and the relentless pursuit of a goal despite overwhelming challenges. His battle with the marlin exemplifies the qualities of hard work, patience and determination. Throughout his struggle, Santiago reflects on his life and his efforts, showing that true success lies not in the outcome, but in the effort and endurance put forth during the process.

subject: Literature in English
q: 'The old man was dreaming about the lions' suggests that he is
explanation: In The Old Man and the Sea, the old man, Santiago, dreams of lions playing on the beaches of Africa, which he had seen when he was a young man. The lions symbolise his enduring spirit, hope and strength, despite his physical struggles. Santiago's dreams of the lions suggest that he remains determined and hopeful, despite the hardships he faces.

subject: Literature in English
q: The novel is set in the
options: white peaks of the Canary Islands
explanation: The Old Man and the Sea is set in a small fishing village near Havana, Cuba. Santiago, the old fisherman, lives in this village, and much of the story takes place on the waters off the coast as he embarks on his journey to catch the great marlin.

subject: Literature in English
q: The plot of the novel revolves around
options: the adventure of a great fish
a man's adventure on the sea
a man's fight with a great fish
a boy's fight with a great fish
explanation: In The Old Man and the Sea, the central plot revolves around Santiago, the old man, and his epic adventure on the sea. The story's climax is his epic struggle with a giant marlin. He battles the fish for three days but ultimately faces defeat when the fish is eaten by sharks.

subject: Literature in English
q: The author's thematic concern is
options: the boy's assistance to the man
the achievements of the old man
the ability to rise above defeat
explanation: In The Old Man and the Sea, the author's thematic concern revolves around the themes of struggle, perseverance and the ability to rise above defeat, even in the face of overwhelming odds. The old man, Santiago, fights the marlin for three days, and although he loses the fish to sharks, his determination, strength and resilience in the face of failure demonstrate his capacity to rise above defeat. This theme of persistence and the human spirit's ability to endure is central to the novel.

subject: Literature in English
q: The skeleton of the fish is brought by the shore to
options: draw sympathy for the old man
show the old man's fruitless effort
show that the skeleton itself is useful
mock the misadventure of the old man
explanation: In The Old Man and the Sea by Ernest Hemingway, the skeleton of the fish brought to the shore symbolises the culmination of the old man's struggle. It is bought up to the shore to draw sympathy for the old man in the readers. The readers understand the amount of effort that he has put into catching the fish, only for it to be eaten by sharks.

"Hurrah for Thunder" by Christopher Okigbo could be regarded as a call to
options: beware of the animals in the jungle
beware of the effects of thunder
condemn the destructive qualities of elephants
be conscious and vigilant of man's abuse of power
explanation: In "Hurrah for Thunder" by Christopher Okigbo, the poem can be interpreted as a reflection on the destructive power of nature, but it also contains symbolic elements that allude to the destructive power of human actions, particularly in the context of societal or political abuse. The thunder, which can be seen as a force of nature, may represent the raw, unbridled power that humans sometimes wield, often with disastrous consequences.

explanation: This extract in Wole Soyinka's "Telephone Conversation," illustrates the tension and friction between the landlady and the tenant. explanation: In Wole Soyinka's Telephone Conversation, the dominant literary technique used is alliteration. Alliteration is the repetition of initial consonant sounds in a poem. Soyinka makes use of alliteration in the poem to make it more rhythmic and memorable.

David Rubadiri's "Stanley meets Mutesa" symbolically describes the meeting between
options: a black king and a white man
explanation: In Stanley Meets Mutesa, David Rubadiri uses the meeting between the European explorer, Henry Morton Stanley, and the African king, Mutesa, as a symbol of the encounter between the West (represented by Stanley) and Africa (represented by Mutesa). This symbolic meeting represents the colonial encounter, where Western powers, through exploration and imperialism, imposed their presence and influence on African societies.

The pervasive mood of the speaker in Mtshali's 'Nightfall in Soweto' is that of
explanation: In Nightfall in Soweto by Mtshali, the mood is one of terror and insecurity. The speaker describes the unsettling atmosphere of nightfall in Soweto, where the darkness evokes feelings of fear and vulnerability due to the social and political conditions of apartheid South Africa. The oppressive environment of the area is depicted through the imagery of darkness and the threats posed by the night.

explanation: In "When This Carnival Finally Closes" by David Mapanje, the poem is written in free verse, meaning it does not follow a specific rhyme scheme or meter. Free verse allows for more fluid expression and is often used to reflect the themes of liberation and the irregularity of life's experiences, which is characteristic of Mapanje's poetry.

The dominant images in Niyi Osundare's "They Too are the Earth" are
explanation: In Niyi Osundare's poem "They Too Are the Earth", the dominant images are poverty, suffering and the downtrodden. The poem portrays the struggles of the maginalised, their suffering and their rightful place in the world. Osundare speaks out for those who are oppressed, portraying them as being integral to the earth itself, despite their hardships.

In these lines from Ojaide's "The Owl Wake Us", the mood is that of a
explanation: In the lines "It is not yet dawn / And we wake from one nightmare / To another," the mood conveyed is one of ongoing hardship and suffering. The speaker reflects a sense of unrelenting distress, where one troubling situation gives way to another, without any respite. This portrays a vicious cycle of oppression, where they cannot escape from their struggles.

In Browning's "My Last Duchess'" the poet persona's attitude toward the Duchess is that of
explanation: In "My Last Duchess", the speaker, the Duke of Ferrara, reveals his deep scorn for his late wife, the Duchess. Throughout the poem, the Duke speaks of her in a detached and cold manner and criticises her for being too kind, too flirtatious and not showing him the proper respect. His attitude is not one of love or sadness, but rather disdain and possessiveness as he discusses how he had her silenced (presumably through her death) because she did not live up to his expectations.

In "Ode on a Grecian Urn," John Keats is concerned with
options: the impermanence of the Grecian Urn
explanation: In "Ode on a Grecian Urn," John Keats reflects on the nature of art and its ability to preserve beauty and emotions across time. The poem contrasts the fleeting nature of human life with the timeless, unchanging beauty captured in the scenes depicted on the urn.

subject: Literature in English

This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The term for the moral flaw or weakness that leads to the downfall of a major character in drama is
explanation: Hamartia is the tragic flaw or moral weakness in the hero of a tragic work that leads to their downfall or misfortune in a tragedy. It is a key concept in classical drama, especially in Greek tragedies.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The figure of speech in which a character makes a statement that has or would have deep and serious implications in the play is
explanation: Euphemism is a figure of speech that helps to soften the blow or impact of an otherwise deep and serious subject matter e.g "He kicked the bucket" is a euphemism for he died. When a character makes a statement in a mild way about something that is deep or has serious implication, for the event that unfolds in the play, euphemism has been used.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A character who does not develop or experience change in the course of his or her existence in a novel is a
explanation: A flat character is one who does not undergo significant development or change throughout the story. They have one or two traits and are not complex or fully developed. In contrast to round characters, flat characters tend to remain static and predictable.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A panegyric poem is composed to
explanation: A panegyric is a type of poem or speech that is composed to praise someone or something, often in an elaborate or formal way. Panegyric poems are written to celebrate the virtues, achievements or qualities of a person, a group or even an idea.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The narrator in a prose work who is also a character is
explanation: A participatory narrator is one who is also a character within the story. This narrator not only tells the story but also plays a role in the events. This is commonly seen in first-person narratives where the narrator uses "I" and is involved in the actions of the story.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A primary ballad is associated with
explanation: A primary ballad is a traditional form of folk song that originated in rural communities. It uses simple, direct language and tells a story about everyday life, love or historical events. These ballads were passed down orally and were usually sung by rural folk.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A trilogy is the
options: sequence of three one-act drama written by the same author
set of three plays written by the same authors
collection of three poems of equal length
series of related stories divided into three equal parts
explanation: This is the correct definition of a trilogy. A trilogy is typically a series of three works that are related in theme, plot or characters and are often divided into three parts with each part contributing to the overarching story.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A novel which focuses on the adventures of a rogue who does not change much in the course of the story is
explanation: The picaresque novel focuses on the adventures of a picaro—a roguish, often lower-class protagonist—who embarks on a series of adventures. The key feature of a picaresque novel is that the protagonist does not undergo significant character development or change throughout the story.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The term given to a type of incident or device which recurs frequently in Literature is
explanation: A motif is a recurring element, theme or incident in a literary work, such as a repeated image, idea or symbol. It often plays a significant role in developing the theme or message of the work.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

Using the name of one thing for something else with which it is closely associated is an instance of
explanation: Metonymy is a literary device where one thing is used to refer to something closely associated with it. For example, using "the crown" to refer to the monarchy or "the White House" to refer to the President or the U.S. government.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

Mock-heroic poetry elevates
options: the beauty in human relationships as exemplified in Homer
trivial subject-matter by using the style of the classical epic
the stripping off of appearances in a witty manner
the important tales of heroes of the past era
explanation: Mock-heroic poetry is a satirical form of poetry that imitates the grand style of classical epic poetry (such as Homer's Iliad or Odyssey), but it applies this elevated style to trivial or mundane subjects. The contrast between the high style and low subject matter creates humour or irony.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

Lineation refers to
options: tracing family descent of poeple in verse
the unit in the rhythmic structure of verse
the arrangement of lines in verse form
the grouping together of a number of units of rhythm
explanation: Lineation is the way in which lines of poetry are arranged or structured in a poem. It deals with how the lines are broken up, the use of stanzas and how the poet decides to organize the text visually and rhythmically.

subject: Literature in English
q:

'He would hear the heavy uneven breathing of the child. It was as if she were carrying a weight with great effort up a long hill...He prayed again ''Father, look after her. Give her peace...Take away my peace forever, but give her peace''

Graham Greene, The Heart of the Matter.

The man's reactions to the presence of the dying child show that he is
explanation: The man’s reactions—his awareness of the child's suffering ("carrying a weight with great effort up a long hill") and his heartfelt prayer ("Take away my peace forever, but give her peace")—demonstrate deep empathy and concern for the child’s well-being. This selflessness and desire to ease her pain reflect compassion.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

'In Umuaro it is not our custom to refuse a call, although we may refuse to do what the caller asks.  Ezeulu does not want to refuse the white man's call and so he is sending his son!'

Chinua Achebe, Arrow of God

The lines above illustrate the use of
explanation: In the lines provided, Achebe uses a statement about custom—"In Umuaro it is not our custom to refuse a call, although we may refuse to do what the caller asks"—to convey a deeper meaning. This is the essence of a proverb; it expresses wisdom or cultural norms. The proverb is extended through the reference to Ezeulu's actions, showing how he adheres to the custom but also manipulates it to avoid confrontation. Traditional parables are longer stories that teach a moral lesson.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

'Those who have nothing but guns for the hungry and think of nothing but death and dying let them spend our earth's fortune harvesting blood from the fields of war. The last banquet shall be their children's blood.

Kofi Anyidoho, "Blood Harvest"

The stanza above succinctly presents the
explanation: The stanza strongly critiques war and violence. It portrays the destruction and loss of life that it brings. The line "harvesting blood from the fields of war" metaphorically represents the brutal and bloody consequences of war and violence. It also shows the devastating impact of war on future generations, as shown by the reference to "the last banquet shall be their children's blood." This highlights the central issue of war and violence and its destructive consequences.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

'Thus; quixoting till a cast-off of my land I sing and fare, person to loved-one pressed braced for this pressure and the captor's hand that snaps off service like a weathered strand...'

Dennis Brutus 'A Troubadour I Traverse'

In the lines above, the poet-persona expresses
explanation: The lines suggest a stance of resistance or struggle. The phrase "quixoting till a cast-off of my land" evokes an image of someone persistently fighting for a cause, even if it seems futile, similar to Don Quixote's battle against imaginary foes. The "captor's hand that snaps off service like a weathered strand" further suggests a refusal to yield or submit easily. This portrays defiance in the face of oppression.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

Africa my Africa of proud warriors in ancestral savannahs...
I have never known you but your blood flows in my veins
Your beautiful black blood that irrigates the fields....

David Diop, "Africa"

In the lines above, Diop uses
explanation: A metaphor is a figure of speech that directly compares two things without using "like" or "as." In the lines "Your beautiful black blood that irrigates the fields," the blood of Africa is metaphorically compared to something that nourishes or brings life, much like water irrigates fields. The comparison between blood and irrigation is symbolic of the life force and vitality of Africa, making this a metaphor.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

Linda's words above express a feeling of
explanation: Linda’s questions "Why? What happened?" and "Did something happen, Willy?" suggest that she is fearful or anxious about the situation. Her reaction shows concern about Willy's well-being and the circumstances that led to his return.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

Willy's first words show that his coming back is
explanation: Linda's use of "Why? What happened?" and "Did something happen, Willy?" suggests she was surprised by his return.

subject: Literature in English
q:
'Symbol of fruitfulness, symbol of barrenness Mother and destroyer, the calm and the storm!
Life and desire and dreams and death
Frank Collymore, Hymn to the Sea,

The address to the sea in the lines above is done by the use of
explanation: An apostrophe is a figure of speech in which a speaker directly addresses someone or something that is not present or is an abstract concept, often treating it as though it were a person. In the given lines, the speaker addresses the sea directly, referring to it as a "symbol of fruitfulness," "barrenness," "Mother," "destroyer," and other qualities. The sea is personified and spoken to as if it can hear and respond.

subject: Literature in English
q:
'When he gave me the bottle, and I opened it, I was shocked by the odour that hit my nose.
I immediately corked it again, and spat on the floor.'
Isidore Okpewho, The Last Duty.

The reaction in the passage above is that of
explanation: The strong negative response to the odour, immediately corking the bottle, and spitting on the floor suggests a physical reaction of disgust or nausea to the smell.

subject: Literature in English
q:
'CYCLIST gets down and begins to prop his bicycle,
CYCLIST: All right. If you're sure it won't take long.
BARBER: I am known for my lightning clippers Even the soldiers know me. I can shave the head of an entire battalion between one coup and the next.
sit down and relax your back. Cycling is not easy when you've abandoned it for some time...
Wole Soyinka, The Beautification of Area Boy.

The literary devices in the dialogue above are
explanation: Hyperbole is an exaggerated statement that is not meant to be taken literally. In the dialogue, the Barber says, "I can shave the head of an entire battalion between one coup and the next." This is clearly an exaggeration to emphasize the speed at which he works. Allusion is a reference to something outside the immediate text. The Barber gives a famous reference to the era when coups succeeded one another in Nigeria's history.

subject: Literature in English
q:
'For seven days it rained that June;
A storm half out to sea kept turning around like a dog trying to settle himself on a rug; We were the fleas that complained in his hair.'
John Updike, Wash

The images set in the lines above are of
explanation: The passage uses vivid imagery to convey a sense of discomfort and a looming threat of destruction. The storm "kept turning around like a dog trying to settle himself on a rug," which suggests the storm's restlessness and chaotic nature. Additionally, the comparison of people to "fleas that complained in his hair" evokes a sense of irritation and helplessness in the face of a powerful and uncontrollable force (the storm).

subject: Literature in English
q:
'We have been asleep, Mr. Speaker, my fellow citizens; asleep in our dream of security! Asleep in our well-meaning, sportsmanlike way of wishing the other fellow well. Asleep in the false security of accepting all men of goodwill who would be free as men of honor.'
Ralph Ellison, Juneteenth.

The force of the speech above is sustained on.
explanation: In the passage, the phrase "asleep" is repeated multiple times to emphasise the speaker's point. This repetition creates a rhythmic, almost hypnotic effect, reinforcing the idea that society has been complacent or unaware of the real dangers or challenges. The repeated use of "asleep" helps to underline the speaker's urgency and frustration, making the speech more powerful and impactful.

subject: Literature in English
q:
'Thomas Gradgrind, sir. A man who proceeds upon the principle that two is not to be talked into allowing for anything over.'
Charles Dickens, Hard Times

From the passage above, Thomas Gradgrind is
explanation: Dogmatic refers to someone who strongly adheres to a set of principles and is unwilling to consider any alternatives or deviations. In the passage, Thomas Gradgrind is described as a person who strictly follows rigid principles (in this case, mathematical precision), implying he is inflexible in his thinking.

subject: Literature in English
q:
'Jose Palacios, his oldest servant, found him floating naked with his eyes open in the purifying waters of his bath and thought he had drowned.
He knew this was one of the many ways the General meditated, but the ecstasy in which he lay drifting seemed that of a man no longer of this world.'
Gabriel Garcia Marquez, The General in His Labyrinth.

The master in the passage above is obviously in a state of
explanation: The passage describes the master floating naked in the bath with his eyes open, showing signs of meditative ecstasy and drifting, which are indicative of a trance-like state where the person is deeply absorbed or lost in thoughts or sensations. This state is further emphasized by the description of the ecstasy in which the master is lying, suggesting a detachment from the physical world and a heightened state of consciousness or meditation.

subject: Literature in English
q: The character 'boy' is used alongside 'the old Man' in the novel to demonstrate.
options: the humiliation that is in old age
different types of characters in the story
the failing productivity that accompanies old age
explanation: In The Old Man and the Sea, the character of the boy is a contrast to the old man (Santiago) and symbolizes both the old man's past vitality and the reality of his current situation. The relationship between Santiago and the boy illustrates the failing productivity that accompanies old age because Santiago is no longer able to provide for himself or catch fish as he once did.

subject: Literature in English
q: The skeleton of the marlin implies the
explanation: In The Old Man and the Sea, the skeleton of the marlin symbolises the futility of human struggle. Santiago's heroic battle to catch the fish and his ultimate defeat by the sharks demonstrate the fleeting nature of success and the inevitability of loss, despite one's best efforts. The marlin's skeleton, once beautiful and strong, now remains a symbol of Santiago's failed attempt to preserve his victory.

subject: Literature in English
q: The Old Man and the Sea epitomises
options: the relationship between a man and a fish
explanation: The Old Man and the Sea is seen as a story about the human struggle against nature, symbolized by Santiago's battle with the marlin. While Santiago’s effort and determination are heroic, his ultimate loss of the fish to the sharks suggests the futility and the transient nature of human endeavours.

subject: Literature in English
q: Santiago loses his catch to the sharks because
options: he does not sail home early
explanation: In The Old Man and the Sea, Santiago catches a giant marlin, but during his struggle to bring it in, the fish is wounded. The blood from the marlin attracts sharks, which eventually attack the fish. Despite Santiago’s efforts to fend off the sharks, they devour the marlin, leaving Santiago with little to show for his efforts.

subject: Literature in English
q: Laye's stay with Uncle Mamadou reveals a lot about
options: his unwillingness to stay with his parents
the strength of communal living in Africa
explanation: In The African Child, Camara Laye's stay with Uncle Mamadou showcases the traditional African practice of communal living, where extended families and the community play an important role in raising children.

subject: Literature in English
q: The setting of The African Child is a kingdom with
explanation: In The African Child by Camara Laye, the setting of the story reflects a combination of Islamic culture and the influences of modernity. The story is set in a traditional and predominantly agrarian community undergoing influences from Islam and France.

subject: Literature in English
q: Laye's first-person narrative techinique
options: quickens the tempo of his narrative
explanation: In The African Child, Camara Laye uses a first-person narrative technique, where the protagonist recounts his experiences directly. This approach provides an intimate perspective, allowing readers to connect with the character's personal emotions, thoughts and experiences.

subject: Literature in English
q: Pa Noble became a living legend because
options: several conflicting stories were woven around him
he attained the status of a first-class citizen
he was the only successful son of a Benin chief
he was rich and very generous
explanation: In Second Class Citizen by Buchi Emecheta, Pa Noble is a character surrounded by various stories, some of which are exaggerated or conflicted. These stories, along with his larger-than-life image, contribute to his status as a "living legend." The different narratives about him, which blend fact with fiction, elevate his reputation in the community, making him more than just a man, but a legendary figure.

subject: Literature in English
q: The marriage of Adah and Francis collapses because of
explanation: The collapse of Adah and Francis's marriage is primarily due to external pressures on the couple. Throughout the novel,  Adah and Francis face numerous challenges and obstacles that put a strain on their relationship. These external pressures include societal expectations, financial difficulties, and the impact of cultural and racial discrimination. These factors contribute to the breakdown of their marriage and this highlights the influence of external forces on their personal lives and relationship.

subject: Literature in English
q: The character of Marshal in the play shows that
explanation: In Morountodun, the character of Marshal represents the pursuit of power. Throughout the play, Marshal's actions and motivations reveal a deep desire for control and influence, particularly over others. His character is driven by the need for power, which influences his decisions and his relationships with other characters.

subject: Literature in English
q: The sub-plots of the play are united by
options: a common concern for the heroine
explanation: In Morountodun, the various sub-plots are united by their focus on the central character, Morountodun. These sub-plots revolve around the heroine's struggles, including her journey, her relationships and the larger societal issues she faces. The attention to her character ties together the different threads of the play, making her the central figure around which the action and various conflicts unfold.

subject: Literature in English
q: The war in Femi Osofisan's Morountondun symbolizes the struggle between the
explanation: In Morountodun by Femi Osofisan, the war symbolizes a broader societal struggle between the ruling class (bourgeoisie) and the working class (proletariat). This conflict is evident in the play, where the exploitation and oppression of the working class by those in power are central themes.

subject: Literature in English
q: The frequent intervention of the Director makes the stage experience of Morountodun very
explanation: In Morountodun, the director’s frequent interventions serve to break the traditional boundaries of a stage performance, making the experience less formal and more interactive.

subject: Literature in English
q:
Not yet old enough for a man, nor young enough for a boy; as a squash is before 't is almost an apple; 't is with him in standing water, between boy and man. He is very well-favoured, and he speaks very shrewishly; one would think his mother's milk were scarce out of him.

The images in the quotation above express the speaker's
explanation: In this passage from Twelfth Night, the speaker (Feste) describes someone who is in the awkward phase between childhood and adulthood. The use of "squash" and "codling" (unripe fruits) as metaphors highlights how the person is neither fully a boy nor a man. The speaker's comment on the person's "shrewish" speech and the suggestion that "his mother's milk were scarce out of him" expresses disdain and a lack of respect.

subject: Literature in English
q:
Come away, come away, death,
And in sad cypress let me be laid...

In the lines above, the character is
options: expressing his preference for a cypress coffin
explanation: The lines you refer to are from Feste's song in Twelfth Night, where the character expresses sorrow over love that is not returned. The use of "Come away, come away, death" reflects the speaker's deep despair, wishing for death as an escape from the pain of unrequited love. The mention of being laid in "sad cypress," a tree often associated with mourning, reinforces the theme of sadness and loss, rather than a preference for death itself or a cypress coffin.

subject: Literature in English
q:
Duke:
Be clamorous, and leap all civil bounds,
Rather than make unprofited return.

Duke's statement above to Cesario means that he should
options: jump the fence to see Olivia
shout to be seen and heard by Olivia
fortify himself against any denial to see Olivia
explanation: In this line, the Duke is urging Cesario (Viola in disguise) to take bold action in his pursuit of Olivia. The phrase "be clamorous" suggests making a loud or forceful approach to capture Olivia's attention, and "leap all civil bounds" implies disregarding social norms or restrictions in order to achieve the goal. Therefore, the Duke is encouraging Cesario to be conspicuous and assertive in a way that will ensure Olivia notices him, even if it means disregarding conventional social boundaries.

subject: Literature in English
q: The traditional verse form of speech as dialogue in Twelfth Night is used to symbolize the
options: function of poetry in the play
explanation: In Twelfth Night, Shakespeare uses verse (typically iambic pentameter) for more elevated, serious or formal moments, often when characters like Orsino or Viola speak. Prose is used for more casual, comedic, or lower-class characters (like Sir Toby or Feste). This distinction helps to highlight differences in social class of the characters.

subject: Literature in English
q: Twelfth Night is preoccupied with the juxtaposition of
explanation: Twelfth Night explores the theme of appearance versus reality, where characters often disguise themselves, leading to confusion and mistaken identities. This theme runs throughout the play, particularly in the cases of Viola and Cesario, and the humorous situations that arise from the characters' misperceptions and misunderstandings.

subject: Literature in English
q: In Niyi Osundare' s ' They Too Are The Earth', the musical effect is achieved by
explanation: Repetition is a key technique used by Osundare in this poem to create rhythm and musicality. The repeated use of words or phrases enhances the poem's musicality and places emphasis on the parts of the poem that convey the theme.

subject: Literature in English
q: The rhyming pattern in the last stanza of Okigbo' s Hurrah for Thunder' makes the stanza
explanation: The last stanza of Okigbo's "Hurrah for Thunder" is a couplet. A Couplet is a pair of lines that usually rhyme.

subject: Literature in English
q: In Keats ' Ode on a Grecian Urn', the recurrent use of the rhetorical question in the lines above suggests
explanation: In Keats' Ode on a Grecian Urn, the recurrent use of the rhetorical question in the lines above suggests admiration and amazement. The rhetorical questions in the poem are used by the speaker to reflect on the timeless beauty and mystery of the scenes depicted on the urn. These questions express the speaker's awe and fascination with the urn's ability to capture eternal moments, frozen in time.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The most important concepts in poetry are
explanation: While elements like plot and character are more prominent in narrative forms like fiction or drama, poetry relies heavily on rhythm (meter, rhyme, and other sound patterns) and imagery (vivid and descriptive language) to convey emotions, ideas and experiences.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The literary device which anticipates that an event will take place is best described as
explanation: Foreshadowing is a technique used by writers to give hints or clues about what will happen later in the story. It helps build suspense and prepares the audience for upcoming events.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The flat character in a literary work
options: is the same as the round character
cannot be found in an allegory
explanation: A flat character is a simple, one-dimensional character who embodies a single trait, quality or idea. Such characters do not undergo significant development or growth throughout the story. They are often used to support the plot or highlight aspects of more complex characters.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The overwhelming pride that destroys the tragic hero is called
explanation: Hubris refers to excessive pride or arrogance in a character, often a tragic hero, which leads to their downfall. This concept is a hallmark of classical tragedy, where the hero's inability to recognise their flaws or limitations causes catastrophic consequences.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A farce refers to a drama that has elements of the
explanation: A farce is a type of comedy that uses exaggerated and absurd situations, improbable events and physical humour to entertain the audience. It often relies on slapstick, mistaken identities and ridiculous scenarios for comedic effect.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

Catharsis is experienced
options: after one has written a play
when a play is still being staged
explanation: Catharsis occurs while the play is being staged, particularly during the moments of heightened emotional intensity, such as the climax and resolution. Audience members experience a release of emotions as they witness the unfolding tragedy or intense drama. This emotional engagement happens in real-time as the events resonate with the audience's fears and sympathies.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

An extended fictional narrative which is realistic is known as a
explanation: A novel is a long, fictional narrative that explores realistic characters, settings and events and delves deeply into themes and human experiences. A Diary refers to a personal, non-fictional record of daily events and thoughts. A Novella is a shorter fictional narrative than a novel but longer than a short story. A Short story is a brief fictional narrative focusing on a single event or character.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The technique in which both the stage and the auditorium are involved in a lay production is called
explanation: Audience participation involves direct interaction with the audience in the auditorium as the play is acted on stage.

subject: Literature in English
q: The theme of Browning's My Last Duchess' is
options: the pitfalls of a duchess now dead
the pride and arrogance of an aristocrat
explanation: The Duke, the speaker in the poem, reveals his pride, arrogance and obsessive desire for control as he narrates the story of his late wife. He views her as a possession rather than a person and values her only because of how she reflects on his status and authority. His disdain for her independent expressions of joy and his chilling implications about her fate underscore his hubris and authoritarian nature.

subject: Literature in English
q: Tanure Ojaide's The Owl Wakes Us portrays a society that is
explanation: In Ojaide’s poem, the presence of the owl and its call awakens us to an impending doom on society due to societal instability.

subject: Literature in English
q: In Mapanje's When This Carnival Finally Closes', the poet is describing the
options: role of spirits in a carnival
role of drumming and dancing in a carnival
role of sycophancy and bad leadership
explanation: The poem is a metaphorical commentary on the political situation, where the "carnival" symbolises the chaotic, superficial and corrupt nature of leadership. The speaker reflects on the end of this "carnival" as a moment of reckoning, hinting at the negative effects of sycophancy, exploitation, and misgovernance, which have been prominent throughout the event.

subject: Literature in English
q: From the tone of Wole Soyinka's 'Telephone Conversation', the two persons talking hold each other in
explanation: The poem explores a telephone conversation between a prospective tenant (the speaker) and a landlady. The landlady's reaction to the speaker's race and her questions reveal a sense of suspicion and prejudice, while the speaker's responses indicate discomfort and unease. Both characters display a lack of trust and understanding towards each other, characterized by mutual suspicion and subtle conflict.

subject: Literature in English
q: The major theme of Thomas Gray''s Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard'' is that death renders life
explanation: Gray's poem meditates on death and the lives of ordinary people, particularly those buried in the churchyard. It reflects on how death brings equality to all, regardless of social status, and that everyone, whether rich or poor, will eventually face it. The poem suggests that the simple lives of the poor, though uncelebrated, have meaning and dignity in the face of death. It is a reflection on the shared human experience of mortality.

subject: Literature in English
q: Browning's My Last Duchess apparently shows the Duke's
explanation: The poem is written to praise Fra Pandolf, the woman he marries because he his displeased by his late wife.

subject: Literature in English
q: In Rubadiri''s '' Stanley Meets Mutesa'', the poet portrays the hardship experienced by the explorer's party through
explanation:
• The poet uses visual images to describe the difficult physical environment and the appearance of the setting, allowing the reader to see the hardships the explorers face.
  • verbal images are used to evoke the physical sensations of speech.

subject: Literature in English
q: John Keats' 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' expresses the idea that
options: every work of art is beautiful
life is long, art is longer
explanation: In the poem, Keats reflects on the timeless nature of art, symbolized by the Grecian urn. The urn preserves an image of eternal beauty and perfection, untouched by the passage of time. The idea that "life is long, art is longer" comes from the notion that while human life is fleeting, art—capturing beauty and meaning—endures far beyond the lives of those who create it.

subject: Literature in English
q:
'The curfew tolls the knell of parting day,
The lowing herd wind slowly o'er the lea,
The plowman homeward plods his weary way;
And leaves the world to darkness and to me.'

In the lines above from Gray's 'Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard', the imagery is
options: of an approaching night in a rustic setting
about a village under a curfew one night
of an approaching death in a village
of a man abandoned in his homeward journey
explanation: The imagery in these lines evokes a tranquil rural scene at twilight, where the "curfew tolls" signalling the end of the day, and various elements of the countryside—such as the "lowing herd" and the "plowman homeward"—paint a picture of a peaceful, quiet evening setting. The transition from day to night and the solitude that follows are key components of the imagery. The image of approaching darkness symbolises the advent of death, which is the central theme of the poem.

subject: Literature in English
q: In Oswald Mtshali's 'Nightfall in Soweto', night
explanation: In the poem, the night is portrayed as a time when the dangers and challenges faced by the people of Soweto are concealed or obscured, allowing evil or danger to creep unnoticed. The "enemy" refers to the social, political, and possibly physical dangers lurking in the darkness, which the night aids .

subject: Literature in English
q: The diction of Femi Osofisan's Morountodun can be described as
explanation: The play addresses societal and political issues, with characters engaging in discussions that reveal the social, cultural and political struggles of the time. The dialogue in Morountodun uses rhetorical devices, persuasive language and impassioned speeches to convey its themes and persuade the audience to reflect on the issues at hand.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation. 

'Careened,
These oily tears
Dripping down the tears on your depressed face,
Will one day be staunched,
I swear!
Ibiwari Ikiriko, Oily Tears

The tone of the poet is
explanation: The poet speaks about tears and depression and expresses a sense of understanding and emotional connection with the subject of the poem. The use of words like "oily tears" and "depressed face" evokes empathy for the emotional pain being described.

subject: Literature in English
q:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation. 

'Lift not the painted veil which those who live call

life: though unreal shapes be pictured there,

And it but mimic all we would believe

With colours idly spread - behind, lurk fear,'
P.B Shelley, Sonnet.

The stanza above is an example of a
explanation: A quatrain is a stanza of four lines, often with a specific rhyme scheme. The stanza contains four lines, making it a quatrain.

The tragic character is the person whose experiences arouse pity and
explanation: In tragic literature, the tragic character is one whose suffering and downfall evoke feelings of pity and terror in the audience. This combination of emotions is especially evident in classical tragedy. This was pointed out by Aristotle in his work Poetics. He describes tragedy as a work that evokes both pity and terror in the mind of the readers. Pity arises from the character’s misfortune. Terror is evoked because the audience realises that the tragic fate could happen to anyone.
